title: validate_track_id_str duplicated in verify.rs (local copy) vs track/mod.rs (canonical)
description: The full track-ID validation algorithm (empty check, first-char check, body char loop, double-hyphen check, trailing-hyphen check, identical error message strings) is implemented twice verbatim within the same crate. `verify.rs` defines a private `validate_track_id_str_local` that is byte-identical to the crate-internal `validate_track_id_str` already present in `track/mod.rs`. A single conceptual change to the validation rules (e.g., allowing dots, changing error wording) would require editing both sites.

locations.summary: pub(crate) fn validate_track_id_str — the canonical implementation, used everywhere else in cli-composition
fn validate_track_id_str_local — private duplicate; only called once at line 169; could instead call crate::track::validate_track_id_str

rationale: Byte-identical 28-line function duplicated in two files of the same crate. Any rule change (valid chars, error messages) must be applied in both places; the second site is already bypassed by every other caller in the module that uses the track-module version.
suggested_fix: Delete `validate_track_id_str_local` from `verify.rs` and replace its single call site (line 169) with `crate::track::validate_track_id_str(&track_id).map_err(|e| format!("invalid track ID: {e}"))?;`.
verify_reason: Confirmed: both `validate_track_id_str` (track/mod.rs lines 12-40) and `validate_track_id_str_local` (verify.rs lines 199-227) are byte-identical 28-line implementations of the same validation algorithm (empty check, first-char check, body-char loop, double-hyphen check, trailing-hyphen check with identical error message strings) in the same crate. A rule change (e.g. allowing dots, changing error text) forces two edits. Genuine exact-clone DRY violation.

A change to the encoding (e.g. switching to u32 length or little-endian) would require edits in two separate crates.

fn append_len_prefixed_bytes — original private implementation in the infrastructure crate

The duplication spans architectural layers (cli-composition → infrastructure), meaning the encoding rule is expressed in two places.
suggested_fix: Make `append_len_prefixed_bytes` pub(crate) or pub in `libs/infrastructure/src/dry_check/corpus.rs` and remove the copy from `corpus_root.rs`, calling the infrastructure version instead.

title: write_manifest re-implements atomic temp-rename write already provided by infrastructure::track::atomic_write
description: The `write_manifest` function in `manifest.rs` (lines 78-95) manually implements the temp-file + rename atomic write pattern (allocates a `{path}.tmp` path, writes to it, then renames). This encodes the same knowledge as `infrastructure::track::atomic_write::atomic_write_file`, which is already imported and used correctly in `corpus_root.rs` (line 15, line 140). The manual version in `manifest.rs` additionally omits the fsync steps present in `atomic_write_file`, so the two implementations diverge on durability guarantees.

locations.summary: write_manifest: manual temp (.tmp suffix) + std::fs::rename atomic write — no fsync, no infrastructure utility
write_dry_corpus_root_manifest: uses atomic_write_file from infrastructure::track::atomic_write — the canonical path with fsync

rationale: Two functions in the same dry/ module encode the same atomic-write protocol differently. The divergence in durability (fsync present in one, absent in the other) is a latent correctness risk on systems where write ordering matters. A single conceptual change to the atomic-write policy would require edits in both places.
suggested_fix: Replace the manual temp+rename in `write_manifest` with a call to `infrastructure::track::atomic_write::atomic_write_file`, converting the JSON string to bytes first. This matches the pattern already used by `write_dry_corpus_root_manifest` in the same module tree.
verify_reason: Confirmed: `write_manifest` (manifest.rs lines 78-95) manually implements temp-suffix + rename without fsync. `write_dry_corpus_root_manifest` (corpus_root.rs lines 111-143) delegates to `atomic_write_file` which adds fsync + parent-dir fsync. Both encode the same atomic-write protocol for the same dry-check sidecar family; the divergence in durability guarantees (fsync absent in manifest.rs) is a genuine latent correctness risk. A policy change to atomic-write semantics requires editing both sites.

title: DataRole and ContractRole duplicate Display/TryFrom<&str> scaffolding
description: Both DataRole and ContractRole implement the same three-piece scaffolding pattern: (1) a `variant_name(&self) -> &'static str` method that dispatches on variants, (2) a `fmt::Display` impl that calls `f.write_str(self.variant_name())`, and (3) a `TryFrom<&str>` impl that delegates to `Self::from_str(value)`. The Display and TryFrom blocks are byte-identical in structure (only the type name differs). A single macro or a shared `VariantNamed` helper trait could eliminate this repetition.

ContractRole: variant_name (225-237), Display (239-243), TryFrom<&str> (263-269)

rationale: Two copies of the same three-block pattern. A change to the pattern (e.g., adding a new Display format convention or changing TryFrom error type) must be replicated in both places.
suggested_fix: Introduce a private trait `VariantNamed { fn variant_name(&self) -> &'static str; }` and a single blanket `impl<T: VariantNamed + FromStr<Err=strum::ParseError>> Display for T` and `impl<T: FromStr<Err=strum::ParseError>> TryFrom<&str> for T`. Alternatively, a macro_rules! that emits variant_name, Display, and TryFrom for a given enum.

The `variant_name` methods are also structurally identical (match dispatch on variants returning &'static str). However, the FromStr impls are substantively different (14 variants with factory methods vs 4 simple variants), and Display+TryFrom together total only ~10 lines per type. A shared `VariantNamed` trait would help, but the actual duplicated code is modest. Adjusting to low (medium overstates impact given that only Display and TryFrom are byte-identical, and the variant sets are legitimately distinct).

title: SHA-256 to lowercase hex encoding implemented three times within dry_check
description: The operation 'compute SHA-256 of bytes and return a 64-char lowercase hex string' is implemented independently in three places within the `dry_check` module. `corpus.rs` exposes a public `sha256_hex(data: &[u8]) -> String` using `hash_bytes.iter().map(|b| format!("{b:02x}")).collect()`. `config.rs` contains an inline copy of the same logic (`hash_bytes.iter().map(|b| format!("{b:02x}")).collect::<String>()`). `codex_dry_checker.rs` uses `format!("{hash_bytes:x}")` — a subtly different format specifier that omits zero-padding for individual bytes and could produce a hash shorter than 64 chars for hashes with leading zero bytes, making it a latent bug as well as a duplication.

rationale: Three independent reimplementations of the same one-line operation. The `codex_dry_checker.rs` copy uses `{hash_bytes:x}` (the GenericArray Display impl) which may or may not produce per-byte zero-padded output depending on the sha2 version, making it a latent correctness divergence in addition to a DRY violation. `corpus::sha256_hex` is already public within the module; `config.rs` and `codex_dry_checker.rs` should call it.
suggested_fix: Replace the inline SHA-256 hex encoding in `config.rs` and `codex_dry_checker.rs` with a call to the already-public `crate::dry_check::corpus::sha256_hex`. Alternatively, move `sha256_hex` into a shared utility module (e.g., `crate::codex_common` or a new `crate::util`) to make the sharing intent explicit.
verify_reason: Confirmed: sha256_hex in corpus.rs (lines 117-121) uses per-byte format!("{b:02x}"), config.rs (lines 281-283) contains an inline copy of the same iteration, and codex_dry_checker.rs (lines 259-261) uses format!("{hash_bytes:x}") — a different format specifier applied to the GenericArray. The third copy is a latent correctness risk: while sha2's GenericArray LowerHex impl does produce zero-padded output in current versions, this is an implementation detail not guaranteed by the public API contract, making it a divergence from the canonical implementation. corpus::sha256_hex is already public within the module and should be called by the other two sites. High severity is correct.

In `dry_fix_runner.rs` the function `dry_fix_smoke_test_codex_version` re-implements the same subprocess spawn, combined-output collection, semver extraction (`parse_semver_from_output`) and major/minor check (`parse_major_minor_version`) inline. The semver parser differs slightly (one trims non-digit prefix chars, the other splits on whitespace only) yet both encode the same validation rule: `major > 0 → reject`, `minor < 115 → reject`.

If the minimum version is bumped, both implementations must be updated independently. The slightly different semver parsers also mean the rule could diverge silently on edge-case version strings.
suggested_fix: Move `parse_semver_from_text`, `parse_major_minor`, and the bounds-checking logic into a single shared function (e.g. `infrastructure::process::validate_codex_version`). Both `CodexReviewFixRunner` and `dry_fix_smoke_test_codex_version` delegate to it.

The minimum version constraint (0.115.0) and range policy are a single piece of knowledge. Critically, the semver parsers differ: parse_semver_from_text trims non-digit prefix characters before splitting, while parse_semver_from_output requires all parts to be all-ASCII-digits — the two parsers can diverge on edge-case version strings like 'v0.115.0'. title: 4-source git diff union (merge-base + cached + worktree + untracked) re-implemented for review and dry-check
description: Both `GitDiffGetter` (`libs/infrastructure/src/review_v2/diff_getter.rs`) and `GitDryCheckDiffGetter` (`libs/infrastructure/src/dry_check/diff_getter.rs`) encode the same 4-source diff-collection rule: (1) `git merge-base HEAD <base>`, (2) `git diff … <merge_base> HEAD`, (3) `git diff … --cached`, (4) `git diff …`, (5) `git ls-files --others --exclude-standard`. Both run the same git commands in the same order with the same error-handling pattern. The difference is output format: `GitDiffGetter` collects bare file paths; `GitDryCheckDiffGetter` collects hunk line ranges. The merge-base lookup, the success check, and the `String::from_utf8_lossy(&stdout).trim().to_owned()` extraction are byte-for-byte identical across both files (diff_getter.rs lines 25-37 vs dry_check/diff_getter.rs lines 41-53).

Adding a fifth source or changing the diff-filter would require parallel edits. The merge-base lookup block is byte-identical.
suggested_fix: Extract a shared helper `git_diff_sources(git: &SystemGitRepo, base: &CommitHash) -> Result<impl Iterator<Item=(&str, Output)>, …>` that runs all four git commands and yields labelled outputs. Each adapter then applies its own output-to-domain-type mapping over the shared iterator.

However, the output processing diverges completely (file paths vs hunk line ranges with --unified=0 parsing), so the bulk of the two implementations is intentionally different. The duplication is real but limited mainly to the merge-base block and the conceptual 4-source enumeration policy. Medium severity: the merge-base extraction (~12 lines) could be a shared helper; the rest is justified divergence.
